title: Autologous platelet-rich plasma for topical application as regenerative therapy in dogs
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"f87kl","text":"This protocol describes a step-by-step procedure for the production of autologous platelet-rich plasma for topical application in dogs.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6bpmn","text":"This protocol was used in the following publication:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"171nd","text":"Tambella AM, Attili AR, Dini F, Palumbo Piccionello A, Vullo C, Serri E, Scrollavezza P, Dupré G. Autologous platelet gel to treat chronic decubital ulcers: a randomized, blind controlled clinical trial in dogs. Veterinary Surgery, 43(6), 2014: 726-733. (ISSN: 0161-3499) (DOI: 10.1111/j.1532-950X.2014.12148.x).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":212,"length":18}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2s4es","text":"http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1532-950X.2014.12148.x/full","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":72}],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"268A93A223284DDBA3694158094C727C","url":"http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1532-950X.2014.12148.x/full"}}}}
steps: The platelet-rich plasma (PRP), or the platelet gel (PG, the coagulated form of PRP), is a hemo-component for topical use that could has an autologous or allogeneic origin. It is obtained from the aggregation of a platelet concentrate mixed with calcium and with biological (thrombin) or pharmacological aggregating factors.[1] The topical use of PRP promotes the healing process in both soft tissues[2,3] and orthopaedic conditions.[4,5] A variety of protocols and activating agents have been proposed in recent years. All the following substances are to be considered activating agents: the bovine thrombin, the agonist peptide of the thrombin receptor, the gelling agent ITA (NATREX Technologies, Inc., Greenville, NC), the batroxobin (clotting enzyme isolated from the venom of the snake Bothrops atrox, belonging to the Viperidae family), ascorbic acid, pulse electric field and autologous thrombin.[6-13] Over the last few years, this type of therapy has been significantly expanding in veterinary medicine.[3,4] This protocol was used in the following publication: Tambella AM, Attili AR, Dini F, Palumbo Piccionello A, Vullo C, Serri E, Scrollavezza P, Dupré G. Autologous platelet gel to treat chronic decubital ulcers: a randomized, blind controlled clinical trial in dogs. Veterinary Surgery , 43(6), 2014: 726-733. (ISSN: 0161-3499) (DOI: 10.1111/j.1532-950X.2014.12148.x). http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1532-950X.2014.12148.x/full

Collect autologous whole blood (50 ml) from the jugular vein into a 60mL syringe. Add acid citrate dextrose solution (ACD-A) at a ratio of 1:9 achieving anticoagulation. ACD‐A solution contains sodium citrate bihydrate 22.0 g/L, citric acid monohydrate 8.0 g/L, glucose monohydrate 24.5 g/L in sterile water for injection. Collect additional 10 mL whole blood in two sodium citrate tubes (3.8%), to extract thrombin.
Use a small aliquot of whole blood for complete blood cell count.
For density separation of blood components, transfer the 50 mL specimen to a Falcon tube and spin at 180 units of gravitational force (x g) for 20 min.
Separate plasma and buffy coat layer and transfer in a Falcon tube under aseptic conditions in a laminar flow cabinet.
Spin the plasma and the buffy coat again at 650 x g for 15 min to stratify platelet concentrates (PCs, platelet pellet) in the bottom layer, and platelet poor plasma (PPP) in the supernatant layer.
Discard part of the PPP, leaving in the tube 14mL volume.

Resuspend the platelet pellet in the PPP and transfer the PRP solution into glass Petri capsule dishes. Choose number and size of the petri capsule dishes to be used based on the type of lesion and morphology of the point of application.
Perform cellular count from PRP automatically. Compare the mean platelet concentration in the PRP and in the whole blood.

Spin the whole blood collected in 5 mL sodium citrate tubes at 650 x g for 10 minutes. Mix the plasma fraction with 10% calcium gluconate (446 mEq/L of calcium), at a ratio of 5:1, and incubate at 37°C for 30 min, in an air-jacketed CO 2 incubator. Squash the clot obtained and collect the final supernatant, the thrombin-rich solution.

Activate the PRP by mixing in sterile glass Petri dishes the PRP, the thrombin-rich solution and the calcium gluconate (volumetric ratio 8:1:0.5) gently rotating the dish. The generation of the ready to use PRP in gel form (PG, platelet gel) should be obtained at room temperature within 5-10 minutes.
Perform these laboratory procedures under aseptic conditions in a laminar flow cabinet following Good Laboratory Practice.
Evaluate aerobic, anaerobic and fungal contaminations by bacteriological and mycological exams of the PRP product.
Separate the PG from the Petri dish using sterile tissue forceps and periosteal elevators. Apply the PG in the target site immediately, covering the site of injury as much as possible.
